translations.source: Imiphumela yocwaningo olusha ikhombisa izinhlelo zempilo ziqala ukuba ngcono esikhathini sobhubhane Ngemva kweminyaka emithathu yobhubhane lweCOVID-19 izinhlelo zempilo emazweni seziqale ukukhombisa izinkomba ezinkulu zokuvuseleleka kwezinhlelo zempilo, ngokweWHO umbiko wesikhashana emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lephalisi yomhlaba ekuqhubekeni kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19: Lwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ngokuqala kuka-2023 amazwe abike ukwenzeka kokwehla kokuphazamiseka ekulethweni kwendlela yemisebenzi yezempilo,kodwa kwavezwa isidingo sokutshala kokungalungiswa nokubekezela kakhulu kwikusasa. Kulamazwe angu-139 aphendule kumzuliswane wesine wokuzwakala kocwaningo lweWHO, amazwe abike ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka emisebenzini yekota esilinganisweni. Emazweni angu-84 lapho ukuhlaziya okudumile kungenzeka,iphesenti lemisebenzi ephazamisekile yehlile ngesilinganiso esisuka ku-56% ngoNtulikazi kuya kuMandulo 2020 ngo-23 % ngoLwezi 2022-Masingana 2023.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

"Results of recent research indicate that health systems are beginning to improve during the pandemic. After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries are starting to show significant signs of revitalization, according to the WHO’s interim report in the fourth round of research on the continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: 2022-September 2023. As of early 2023, countries reported a possibility of a decrease in disruptions to the delivery of health services, but emphasized the need for continued investment and resilience for the future. In the 139 countries that responded to the WHO’s fourth round of research findings, countries reported a continuation of disruptions to integrated health services. In 84 countries where a comprehensive analysis was possible, the percentage of disrupted services decreased from 56% in July 2020 to 23% in September 2022-2023."

translations.source: Ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka kungenxa yakho kokubili ukufuneka nokusaplaya izinkinga eziseceleni, okufaka amazinga aphansi okunakekelwa kwezempilo emphakathini okudingayo kanye nomkhawulo wokubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo nezinsizakusebenza ezinye zokunakekelwa kwezempilo njengokuvulwa kwemitholampilo noma ukubakhona kwesitoko semithi nemikhiqizo. "Izindaba ezamukelwayo ukuthi izinhlelozempilo emzaweni amaningi ziqala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ezigidini zabantu abawugejile ngesikhathi sobhubhane," kusho uDkt Rudi Eggers, Umqondisi weWHO weMisebenzi yeZempilo yoKuhlanganisiwe. "Kodwa sidinga ukuqinisekisa ukuthi wonke amazwe ayaqhubeka nokuvala izikhala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo,nokusebenzisa izifundo abazifundile ukwakha izinhlelo zempilo ezizilungiselele nokubekezela kwekusasa". Izimpawu zokuqala eziqaphelekayo ekuvuseleleni Kulolu cwaningo olusha,amazwe ambalwa abike ngokungenhloso isikalo sasemuva sokuthola kuzinkundla zonke umsebenzi wokuletha nokusebenza kwezempilo yomphakathi okubalulekile kusukela 2020-2021 ukubikwa kukhombisa ukubaluleka kwegxathu kubuyelwe emazingeni angaphambi kobhubhane ukulethwa kokusebenza nokusebenza okukhulu kwezinhlelo.
translations.reference: Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"The continuation of disruption is due to both the necessity and the ripple effects of related problems, including low levels of healthcare access in communities in need and the limited availability of healthcare workers and services, such as the opening of clinics or the availability of medicine and supplies. "The encouraging news is that health services in many areas are beginning to revitalize essential health activities for populations that were neglected during the pandemic," said Dr. Rudi Eggers, WHO Director of Integrated Health Operations. "But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close gaps in revitalizing health services and use the lessons they have learned to build health systems that are prepared and resilient for the future." Early signs of revitalization In this new study, a few countries have unintentionally reported the recent occurrence of delivering and operating essential public health work across all areas from 2020-2021, reporting shows the importance of returning to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and the greater effectiveness of health systems."

translations.source: Ekupheleni kuka-2022, amazwe amaningi abike izimpawu ezingacacile zokuvuselelwa kokusebenza okufaka ukusebenza kwezocansi, ukuzala, abakhullwe, abazelwe, izingane nezempilo yentsha, ukudla okunempilo ukugomma,izifo ezithelelanayo (okufaka umalaleveva, iHIV, iTB nezinye izifo ezithelelana ngokocansi) izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe,izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,ukuphathwa kwengqondo, okwengqondo nesifo sokusebenzisa izidakamiza, ukunakekelwa kwabantu abadala nokunakekelwa okulekelelayo nokwesiko. Inombolo yamazwe abika ukuphazamiseka ohlelweni lwawo lesaplayi sheni ezweni okungafika kuhhafu (29 ka-59 wamazwe aphendulayo) okunganekota (18 wa-66 wamazwe) aphedulayo kulonyaka odlule. Ngaphandle kwezinkomba zokuvuselela,ukuphazamiseka komsebenzi kuyaqhubeka emazweni onke namazinga emali engenayo kuzona zonke izinhlelo soomsebenzi wokulatha nokulandelela umsebenzi ezindaweni. Amazwe aphinde abhekane nokunyuka komsebenzi okumele wenziwe - ikakhulukazi emisebenzini yokubheka, ukuxilonga nokwelapha izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo - okungaholela kumiphumela engemihle uma abantu bebambezeleka ukuthola ukunakekelwa ngesikhathi. Ukuvuselelwa kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile kubucayi ngenxa yokuphazamiseka - okufaka imisebenzi yokugqugquzela ezempilo, ukuvinjwa kwezifo, ukwelapha, ukuvuselelwa kwesimilo nephaliyeshini - kungaba nokukhulu umthelela ohlukene wezempil nezinga lomuntu ukunobhubhane qobo ikakhulukazi kubantu babuthaka.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"At the end of 2022, many countries reported signs of a recovery in health services, including sexual and reproductive health, maternal health, newborn and child health, nutrition, immunization, communicable diseases (including measles, HIV, TB, and other sexually transmitted infections), neglected tropical diseases, non-communicable diseases, mental health, psychosocial and substance use disorders, and care for older adults and palliative and traditional care. The number of countries reporting disruptions to their health service delivery systems was significant, reaching as many as half (29 of 59 responding countries) for essential services and nearly half (18 of 66 responding countries) last year. Beyond the signs of recovery, service delivery disruptions continue across countries and income levels in all health programs for data collection and workforce tracking. Countries are also facing an increase in the workload required – particularly in the areas of screening, diagnosing, and treating non-communicable diseases – which can lead to negative outcomes if people experience delays in accessing care. The recovery of essential health services is critical given the disruptions – including health promotion activities, disease prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, and physiotherapy – and can have a significant and varied impact on health and mortality, particularly among vulnerable populations."

translations.source: Ukuhlanganisa imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile elinye igxathu elibalulekile eliya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo noguquko,amazwe amaningi asebe nenqubekela phambili ekuhlanganiseni imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 endleleni yokuletha imisebenzi yezempilo. Cishe u-80%-90% wamazwe ahlanganyele ngokuphelele ekugonyweni kweCOVID-19 ukuxilonga nemisebenzi yokuphathwa kwecala kanye nemisebenzi yesimo sangemuva kweCOVID-19 endleleni yomsebenzi wokuletha. Kuyafana amazwe amaningi (u-80% wamazwe awu-83 aphendulile) abike okungenani okuncane ekukhuphukeni kokuthola amathuluzi abalulekile eCovid-19 (isibonelo ukuxilongwa kweCOVID-19, ukwelaphangxube, imigomo namathuluzi okuzivikela siqu - PPE), ngezinkinga zokusebenza kwezempilo nokwentuleka koxhasomali okumele izivimbo ezijwayeleke kakhulu. Ukuqhubeka kokusekwa kuyadingeka ukuvuselela,ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela Amazwe amaningi aseqalile asebenzisa asebekufundile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19 okufaka ngezikhungo zenamba yokusbenza kokusha ukuphazamisa amasu endleleni yokulethwa kwezempilo.
translations.reference: Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Integrating COVID-19 activities into essential health services represents another crucial step towards system strengthening and transformation. Many countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 activities into the delivery of health services. Approximately 80%-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 activities into the case management and backstopping functions within the health service delivery pathway. A significant number of countries (80% of the 83 respondents) have reported at least some improvement in the availability of essential COVID-19 tools (e.g., COVID-19 testing, treatment, protocols, and personal protective equipment - PPE), facing challenges related to health workforce capacity and supply chain disruptions, which are common issues. Continued support is needed to revitalize, sustain, and prepare. Many countries have begun to utilize what they have learned during the COVID-19 pandemic, including incorporating new operational approaches into the health service delivery pathway, to mitigate disruptions."

**Key notes on the translation:**

*   **Context:** I've tried to maintain the formal and technical tone of the original document, assuming it's likely a report or policy document.
*   **Specificity:** I've kept specific terms like "PPE" and "backstopping" as they are commonly used in the health sector.
*   **Clarity:** I've rephrased some sentences to improve clarity and flow in English.
*   **Numbers:** I've translated the percentages accurately.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka ukubekwa kwezindlela zokwelapha ngefoni,ukugqugquzela ukunakekelwa usekhaya nokungenelela kokuzinakekela, izindlela zokuqinisa ukubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo, amandla nezindlela zokweseka, ukwenza kabusha ekuthengeni nokuletha imithi namasaplayi ezinye izindlela zokuxhumana nomphakathi ukubambisana nabanikezeli bomkhakha ozimele nowomphakathi. Amakota amathathu ezwe abaike ukufakwa koxhasomali olwengeziwe oluya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo lwesikhathi eside, ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela. Amazwe agcizelele isidingo sokwesekwa kweWHO ekubhekaneni nezinkinga ezisele ngokwengqikithi yeCOVID-19 nangale,ikakhulukazi okujwayele ukuhlobana nokuqinisa ukusebenza kwezempilo, ukwakha amandla okubheka imisebenzi yezempilo, ukwakha ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala okuncike kuzindlela zokunakekela, ukubusa, inqubomgomo nokuhlela nokuhlela ezezimali noxhasomali. Inothi kubahleli: Emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lwephasile yomhlaba weWHO, amazwe angu-222, izindawo bamenywa ukuba baphendule kucwaningo oluncike kuwebhu eqinisikesiwe phakathi kukaLwezi 2022 noMasingana 2023. Ucwaningo lwesine lulandele kucwaningo lwephasile edlule yango-2020 no2021 lweWHO: uMzuliswano 1 (Nhlaba-Mandulo 2020) uMzuliswano 2 (Masingana-Ndasa 2021) noMzuliswano 3 (Lwezi-Zibandlela 2021) okwakhombisa ukukhulu bokuthi ubhubhane lalukhahlameza ukuqhubeka kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile nokuthi amazwe enza kanjani.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"This includes the deployment of telephone-based treatment pathways, the promotion of home-based care and self-care interventions, strategies to strengthen the resilience of health workers, resources and support mechanisms, and the revitalization of procurement and delivery of medicines and supplies through alternative communication channels to collaborate with private and public sector providers. Three countries have agreed to incorporate additional Somali to contribute to the long-term system renewal, flexibility, and preparedness. Countries emphasized the need for WHO support in addressing the remaining challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic and beyond, particularly those related to strengthening health systems, building health workforce capacity, establishing primary health care based on care pathways, governance, policy and planning, and financial and technical assistance. Note to editors: In the fourth round of the WHO global pulse survey, 222 countries and territories were invited to respond to a web-based survey between July 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey follows the WHO’s previous pulse surveys of 2020 and 2021: Round 1 (February-March 2020), Round 2 (May-June 2021), and Round 3 (July-September 2021), which highlighted the impact of the pandemic on the continuity of essential health services and how countries were responding."

translations.source: Ngesikhathi ucwaningo lwephasile lunemikhawulo njengokubika ukuchema nokumela,amandla alomzamo ukuthi mukhulu futhi uletha ulwazi ngokuphindekile. Itemu "izwe" kumele liqondwe ukuthi lifaka wonke amazwe, imingcele nezindawo. Abahlaziya okudumile kwaqedwa kuncike ezimpendulweni eziphuma emazweni angu-84, abahlukumezi noma izindawo ezaphendula okungenani kusahluko socwaningo olulodwa kuyona yonke imizuliswano emine yocwaningo.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu text into English:

"While research on resilience has limitations, such as reporting bias and representation, the strength of this effort is that it is extensive and provides repeated insights. The term "country" should be understood to include all nations, borders, and regions. The analysis of popular opinion was completed based on responses from 84 countries, with at least one region from those countries responding to at least one of the four research modules."



I've aimed for a clear and accurate translation, maintaining the original meaning as closely as possible.

translations.source: Isitatimende sokubungaza ukuphela kweminyaka engu-50 yeHRP: iSweden ne-United Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthen Ireland Ukubamba isimo seNqubomgomo neKomidi lokuXhumana (PCC) Amalungu abungaza ukuphela kuka-50 HRP nokuzuza okuningi, iSweden neUnited Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthern Ireland ukufunda ukuqapheleka okulandelayo kokwamukela nokuhalalisa emhlanganweni wesi-36 weHRP PCC. Isizukulwane sethu sesibone inqubekela phambili enkulu kusayensi nezobuchwepheshe ukuthi konke okuqhubekayo kwesizukulwane ndawonye, nokubona okwemfundo okumbalwa,ezobuchwepheshe, ezokwelapha noguquko lwenhlalakahle luqhutshwa ukuguquka kokuvikela kulandelwa ukwenzangcono kwezempilo yokuzala, ukwenza kabusha kwezempilo kanye namalungelo nokulingana ngokobulili. Kepha ukucabanga izingqinamba emkhakheni weSRHR uqhubeke futhi kuphikiswane,okunye ukuqhubeka kusembuzweni futhi namhlanje. Sincoma abasebenzi beHRP ababambisani nabeseki kwabakutholile okuningi kuze kube yinamhlanje nokubona izinto ezinkulu ezenzekile ngesikhathi esinguhhafu wekhulunyaka ekukhiqizeni ubufakazi obeseka ukungena okuphelele nokulinganayo kwezempilo namalungelo ezocansi nokuzala.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

**Statement on the Completion of 50 Years of HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland Regarding the Status of the Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC)**

Members announce the completion of 50 years of HRP and significant gains. Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland note the following for consideration and endorsement at the 36th HRP PCC meeting.

Our generation has witnessed significant progress in science and technology, and all ongoing efforts of our generation, with limited educational, technological, medical, and social transformation advancements, are driven by the shift towards protection, followed by improvements in reproductive health, health restoration, and gender equality rights. However, challenges in the SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) field continue and remain contentious, with some progress remaining in question even today.

We commend the HRP staff who have collaborated with those who have gained much to date and have seen great things happen during this half-century in producing evidence supporting full and equitable access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **HRP:** I've kept "HRP" as is, assuming it's an acronym that would be understood within the context of the document. If you know what it stands for, it could be expanded in the translation.
*   **SRHR:** Similarly, I've kept "SRHR" as is.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English. Zulu can have complex sentence structures, and some nuances are difficult to capture perfectly in translation.
*   **Context:**  Understanding the specific context of the HRP and PCC would allow for even more precise translation.

translations.source: Singathanda ukuveza imikhakha emikhulu emithathu yokuzuziwe Okokuqala ucwaningo olusezingeni eliphezulu lweHRP lube nomthelela obonakalayo kunqubomgomo yezempilo yomphakathi,amazinga, nokwenza emhlabeni wonke okubhekene nezidingo zokuguquka kusibalo sabantu esahlukene. Okwesibili HRP iqapheleke ngeHRP Alliance, isiqinise izikhungo zocwaningpo endaweni nasemhlabeni,kwavumela abacwaningi abaningi ukuthi bathole amakhono okucwabinga ukuqinisekisa ubunhalo obuphezulu bocwaningo lweSRHR ukuthi liqhubeke futhi lunyuke esikhathini esizayo. Okwesithathu,umhlaba uhlelwa yizinkinga, ukuxabana nokunyusa ukuqaba ekulinganeni kobulili, iHRP isize ukubamba isisekelo samalungelo abasefazane nezingane nabantu ababuthaka. I-HRP ihlala iyithuluzi elibalulekile kuhlelo lweUnited Nations yocwaningo lokhukhiqiza kubantu nokuqhubeka nokuba umholi ovelele kwezempilo yezocansi nokuzala emhlabeni.
translations.reference: We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"We are pleased to highlight three key areas of impact for the Human Reproduction Programme (HRP). Firstly, high-quality HRP research has a demonstrable influence on public health policy, standards, and global efforts addressing the diverse needs of populations. Secondly, HRP is recognized through the HRP Alliance, which strengthens research institutions locally and globally, enabling numerous researchers to acquire skills to ensure the quality of SRHR research continues and improves over time. Thirdly, given the world is shaped by challenges, inequalities, and rising discrimination based on gender, HRP has helped to underpin the rights of women, children, and vulnerable people. HRP remains a vital tool within the United Nations system for generating research for people and continuing to be a leading force in sexual and reproductive health globally."

translations.source: HRP nababambisani bayo sebesisondeze kusithembiso sango-1994 KuNkomfa Yamazwe angaphandle Esibalweni Sabantu neNtuthuko, futhi isimemezelo sibeka amalungelo abantu ikakhulukazi abesifazane namantombazane phakathi nokugcineka kwentuthuko. Sifisa ukuzwakalisa ukubonga nokujabula kwethu kunobhala weHRP, ababambisani, kanye nabaxhasi kanye nohlelokuxhumana lomhlaba ezizindeni zokubambisana nongoti abasiza ukwenza isiqiniseko ukuthi imizamo inikela ekwenzeni umehluko kubantu besifazane, amadoda, izingane, intsha, nemiphakathini eyahlukene iHRP izimisele ngokusiza. Ngokubambela amalunga ePCC sivumeleni siqinisekise ukubambisana kwethu nani ukweseka okuzuziwe kweHRP ekuphumeleleni okuncike ku-ajenda 2030.
translations.reference: HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.

translations.source: I-WHO yethula umzamo omusha wokwenza ngcono ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane Ukusiza amazwe ukuthi azilungisele okungcono kwekusasa lobhubhane,WHO yethule umzamo namahlanje ozonikeza imiyalelo yokuhlela ngokuhlanganisa ngokuphendula kunoma iyiphi iphathojini yokuphefumula njengomkhuhlane noma ikhorona. I-Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, noma iPRET, ihlanganisa amathuluzi akamuva nezindlela zokufunda okwabelwana ngazo kanye nesenzo esiyiqoqo esisungulwe phakathi nobhadane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zakamuva zezempilo zomphakathi. Ngomzamo iWHO izosebenzisa indlela yokudlulisela ukuyala amazwe ekuhleleni ubhubhane,ngoba kukhona amandla amaningi namandla amaningi afanayo kumaqoqo ephathojini. I-PRET iphendula isimemezelo somuyalelo wezobuchwepheshe nokwesekwa kokugqugquzelwa nokuqiniswa kokuhlanganisa ukuzilungiselela nokuphendula,njengova kuveziwe kuzinqumo zeWorld Health Assembly.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"WHO introduces a new effort to improve pandemic preparedness. To help countries better prepare for the future of pandemics, WHO today launched an initiative that will provide guidance on planning, including responding to any respiratory disease such as pneumonia or coronavirus. The Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and learning methods shared, and a collection of actions established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through this effort, WHO will use a guidance delivery approach to assist countries in planning for pandemics, as there are many strengths and similar capabilities within the disease response clusters. PRET responds to the call for technical and support expertise to promote and strengthen preparedness and response, as outlined in the World Health Assembly resolutions."

translations.source: Ububhane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zezempilo zikhombisile ukuthi amazwe adinga ukuthi alungele ukubhekana nezinsongo zezifo ezithathelwanayo, ngezinhlelo zokuzilungiselela ezilungiselelwe ngesandla kanye nokuxhumana okungcono nokusebenzisana neminye imikhakha efana nezolimo. "Ukuzilungiselela, ukuvikela nemisebenzi yokuphendula akumele kube yisifundazwe somkhakha wezempilo kuphela," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Njengeziphuthumayo zempilo zinomthelela kumikhakha eminingi, ngakho nokuzilungiselela kwethu nemizamo yokuphendula mayilingane nemikhakah, izinhlobo namagciwane. Kubucayi futhi ukuthi ukukhuluma komphakathi nokulingana kuphakathi kwemizamo yethu, ikakhulukazi kulabo bantu abagcindezelekile futhi abasengcupheni enkulu." I-PRET iwuguquko endleleni yeWHO ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ngokusebenzisa indlela yelensi yokudlulisela, ukunokugxila ezifweni ezithile.
translations.reference: The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"The COVID-19 pandemic and other emerging health crises have demonstrated that countries need to be prepared to deal with infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans and better communication and coordination with other sectors such as agriculture. "Preparedness, prevention and response efforts should not be the sole responsibility of the health sector," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. "As health emergencies impact many sectors, so too must our preparedness and response efforts align with sectors, variants and pathogens. It is also crucial that community engagement and equity are at the heart of our efforts, particularly for vulnerable populations and those at greatest risk." PRET represents a shift in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness by using a lens of transmission, focusing on specific diseases.

translations.source: I-WHO izoqhubeka nokuthuthukisa nokusabalalisa imiyalelo ezifweni ezithile njengoba kudingeka. Ukwethulwa komzamo kuzokwenzeka ngosuku lokugcina lwemihlangano abangaphezu kuka-120 abamele Member State, ababambisani, noNobhala weWHO, ukuchaza indlela eya phambili yePRET okufaka ukuthuthuka kwebalazwe lokwenza lomhlaba kokuzilungiselela lobhubhane lwegciwane lokuphefumula. Imizamo yePRET isifundo sokuqala sigxile kumagciwane pokuphefumula, okufaka umkhuhlane, igciwane lekhorona namagciwane ezokuphefumula. Ngokunikezwa ubhubhane oluqhubekayo nokwesabisa okungenzeka emkhuhlaneni, lemojuli izokwenza amazwe abheke ngokucophelela, ukuhlola, ukubuyekeza imizamo yokuhlela ubhubhane lwezokuphefumula ukuqinisekisa banamandla okusebenzayo, namandla akhona. Uhlelo lusendleleni lokuhlonza iqoqo elilandelayo lamagciwane, njengama-arbovirus, ukuthi kubhekwane nawo ngaphansi kwalomzamo.
translations.reference: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidelines for specific health events as needed. The launch of the initiative will take place on the final day of meetings involving more than 120 representatives from Member States, partners, and the WHO Secretary-General, to outline the way forward for PRET, including the advancement of national action plans for pandemic preparedness for respiratory pathogens. The initial efforts of PRET will focus on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronavirus, and other respiratory pathogens. Given the ongoing pandemic and the potential threat of influenza, this module will encourage countries to carefully review, assess, and update their respiratory pandemic preparedness efforts to ensure they are functional and sustainable. The program is in the process of identifying the next group of pathogens to be addressed under this initiative, such as arboviruses.

translations.source: Lokhu kuzolandela okuhamba phambili okuhlonziwe ngeziphakamiso eziyishumi zokuqinisa ukwakhiwa komhlaba kokuzilungiselela kokuphuthuma kwezempilo, ukuphendula nokubekezela (HEPR). Umzamo wePRET ukuyisa esikhathini esisha sokuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokumela uguquko lwemisebenzi ngqo yeWHO ukweseka wonke amaMember State ekuqiniseni ezempilo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo, ukuvikela namandla okuphendula nokukwazi. Umzamo ungaphinde usize ukusebenzisa izinjongo nokuhlinzekwa kwePandemic Accord okwamanje okusaxoxwa ngayo yiWHO Member State. Kulandela lokho okufakiwe nezingxoxo emihlanganweni yababambe iqhaza, iWHO imema ababambisani kulesimemezelo somhlaba sokwenza ukuwandisa ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane legciwane lokuphefumula. translations.reference: This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action

translations.source: AmaMember State eWHO, nababambisani basebenza ndawonye ukuhlonza nokuhambisa phambili ukwenza izenzo zokuqinisa izwe, isifunda, nokuzilungiselela komhlaba kwezobhubhane nokuvela kwezifo ezithelelanayo ezisabisayo. Siyayibona indima yomphakathi nayo yonke imikhakha sidinga ukulinganisa nokugcina umfutho ekupheleni kwesakile yokusaba nokunganaki lokho kujwayelekile ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane nokuphendula. Sikhumbula izifundo zobhubhane olwedlule ne-ephidemikhi, ikakhulukazi lezo ezidalwa amagciwane okuphefumula. Lawo magciwane azoqhubeka nokuletha ukusabisa komhlaba okukhulu ngamandla okudala izinkinga ezinkulu nokufa, izinhlelo zempilo ezingaphezu kwamandla, ukwehlisa ezomnotho zomhlaba, ukwengeza ukungalingani, okukhona ekungeneni kumathuluzi omabili okuvikela ubhubhane nokunakekelwa ezempilo kubona bonke abantu. Lesi yisimemezelo sokwenza ukuqhubeza ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokuvela kokwesaba emhlabeni.
translations.reference: WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"WHO Member States, and partners work together to strengthen and advance actions for national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious diseases that pose a serious threat. We see the role of society and all sectors needing to align and maintain momentum towards the end of the pandemic fear and disregard the normalcy in pandemic preparedness and response. We remember the lessons of past pandemics and epidemics, particularly those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens will continue to bring great global risk with the power to cause major problems and death, overwhelm health systems, depress the global economy, increase inequality, and exacerbate the existing gaps in access to both pandemic prevention and health care tools for all people. This is a call to action to enhance pandemic preparedness and response to emerging threats globally."

translations.source: Ukunyusa ukuxhumana kubabambisani ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ukuhlela ngezinhlelo zokuxhumana nokusebenzisana. Lokhu kufaka ukwakha izinhlelo ezilinganayo ukwenza imisebenzi ngokuhlanganyela; Nokwabelana ngolwazi ekwenzeni okuhle, izingqinamba kanye namathuba. Ukunikela ngotshalomali olugcinekile, izimali nokubheka kokulungela ubhubhane ngokugxila ekubhekeni izikhala ezihlonze ngesikhathi sobhubhane olwedlule. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 luphendule ukukhombisa ukuthi yikuphi okungazuzwa ngokuzibophezela kwezepolitiki, ukukhuluma komphakathi kanye noxhasomali.
translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Improving communication is crucial in disaster preparedness, involving the planning of communication and collaboration systems. This includes building equitable systems to perform tasks collaboratively; and sharing knowledge to achieve better outcomes, address challenges, and leverage opportunities. Providing sustainable funding, resources, and assessments for disaster preparedness with a focus on identifying gaps experienced during past disasters. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted where commitment from political leaders, public engagement, and support can be beneficial."

translations.source: Phakathi nalomsebenzi ukuqinisekisa ukulingana ukulungela obhubhane olulandelayo ndawonye. Ukwenza komphakathi wonke kuyadingeka ukwenza inqubekela phambili evezwe kule simemezelo sokwenza. Ukwenza kumele kuqinise imiphakathi ebekezelayo; egcinayo nokugcineka nokwakha izindlela zezinhlelo; nokubambisa kwamandla amakhulu kokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula. Lesi simemezelo sokwenza sakhishwa eKuzilungiseleni nokubekezela kokuvela kokusabisayo (PRET): Umhlangano womhlaba wekusasa lobhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula obanjwe ngo-24-26 Mbasa 2023 eGeneva, eSwitzerland.
translations.reference: At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Within this work is the assurance of equality in preparation for the next pandemic together. The involvement of the entire community is necessary to make progress as highlighted in this call to action. The action must reassure vulnerable communities; preserve and maintain sustainability and build pathways for systems; and harness the power of rapid preparedness and response. This call to action was released at the Preventing Epidemics of Respiratory Diseases (PRET): The Global Meeting on Future Pandemics held from 24-26 May 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland."

translations.source: I-WHO inxenxa ukunyuswa kokwenza ngamathuluzi aphakanyisiwe ukulwa nomalaleveva Ukumaka uSuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni, Inhlangano Yezempilo Yomhlaba (WHO) izokhipha isimememzelo sokunyusa ukwenza kokungenelela okukhona nokusha ukusindisa izimpilo kumalaleveva. Okungafika kuzigidi ezingu-1.5 izingane ezisengcupheni enkulu yokugula nokufa okusuka kumalaleveva eGhana, eKenya naseMalawi manje sebethole umgomo wokuqala kamalaleveva, RTS, S/AS01 (RTS,S), siyabonga kuhlelo lokuhlola oluqhubekayo oluxhunyaniswa yiWHO. Ukuhlolwa komgomo wamalaleveva, owethulwe ngo-2019,kwenyusa ukulingana kokuthola ukuvikelwa kumalaleveva kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu futhi kusindisa izimpilo. Ukwenziwe kakhulu, iWHO ilinganisela ukuthi umgomo kamalaleveva ungasingisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane ngonyaka.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

WHO urges increased use of recommended tools to combat malaria. To mark World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) will issue a call to increase the use of existing and new interventions to save lives from malaria. Up to 1.5 million children at high risk of malaria illness and death in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi have now received the first malaria vaccine, RTS, S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme supported by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilot, introduced in 2019, has increased the equity of access to malaria protection for the most vulnerable and saved lives. Furthermore, WHO estimates that the malaria vaccine could save ten thousand children’s lives each year.

translations.source: "Sinamathuluzi ukwehlisa umalaleveva,iphekheji yokungenenela ebandakanya ukulawula ivektha,imithi yokuvikela, ukuhlola nokwelapha," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Lokhu kulandelwa ngumgomo ophephile nosebenzayo kamalaleveva, ongasindisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane unyaka ngamunye. Ngotshalomali olugcinekile nokunyuka kwemizamo ukufika kulabo abasengcupheni kakhulu, ukuqedwa kwamalaleveva emazweni amaningi sekuyafika. "Usuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni 2023 lumakwe ngaphansi kwengqikithi, "Isikhathi sokuwqeda umalaleveva, tshala, yenza kabusha yenza". Kule ngqikithi, iWHO inxenxa okunye ukwenza okusebenzayo kwamathuluzi akhona namasu okuvikela, ukuxilonga nokwelapha umalaleveva, ikakhulukazi kulesosibalo esingcindezelekile.
translations.reference: “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"We have the tools to reduce measles, a package including vector control, vaccines, surveillance, and treatment," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "This is followed by a safe and effective measles strategy, which can save millions of children's lives each year. With sustained funding and increased efforts to reach the most vulnerable, measles elimination in many countries is now within reach." World Measles Day 2023 is marked under the theme, "Time to end measles, vaccinate, revitalize, rebuild." Under this theme, WHO urges for greater use of existing tools and prevention measures, measles surveillance and treatment, especially in vulnerable populations.

translations.source: Ngokombiko wokugcina wamalaleveva emhlabeni,oshicilelwe ngoZibandlela 2022, bekunesilinganiso sika-247 wezigidi zamacala amasha kamalaleveva ngo-2021. I-WHO African Region iyaqhubeka ukuthwala umthwalo onzima wesifo- okuballelwa esilinganiswenisa ku-95% wamacala kamalaleveva (u-234 wezigidi) no-96% wokufa (593 000) ngo-2021. Kungafika ku-80% ukufa okudalwa umalaleveva eSifundeni se-Afrika obukukubantwana abangaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Amasu amasha namathuluzi Amazweasenze inqubekela phambili ekwandiseni ukungena kumisebenzi yamalaleveva kusibalo sabantu esisengcupheni kakhulu.
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

According to the final malaria report worldwide, published by WHO in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to bear the brunt of the disease, accounting for 95% of malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of deaths (593,000) in 2021. Up to 80% of malaria-related deaths in the African Region affect children under five years of age. New tools and strategies have enabled countries to make progress in increasing access to malaria interventions among the most at-risk populations.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kokuqhubeka abantu abaningi abasengcupheni yamalaleveva basantula ukungena emisebenzini engavimba, noma itthole bese yelapha isifo. Izingqinamba ekwandeni kokungena kumisebenzi kamalaleveva sezihlanganisisiwe,ikakhulukazi esu-Saharan Afrika, ngobhubhane lweCOVID-19, ukujika izinkinga zobuntu, uxhasomali olunezivimbo, izinhlelo zokubheka ezintekenteke, ukwehla ekwenzeni kwamathuluzi okulwa ngqo nomalaleveva. Ukubhekana nalokhu okusabisayo nokweseka amazwe ekwakheni izinhlelo zokubekezela zamalaleveva, iWHO isanda kushicilela amasu amasha nomhlahlandlela: isu elisha lelinesidakamizwa esingenamalaleveva ukuvika eAfrika; umzamo omusha wokumisa ukusabalala kweAnophelesi stephensi ezindaweni zasemadolobheni;
translations.reference: Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English:

"Despite the ongoing situation, many people vulnerable to malaria are struggling to access employment or receive treatment for the disease. Barriers to increasing access to malaria interventions are compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, social and political instability, gender inequality, unreliable health systems, and a decline in the effectiveness of direct malaria control tools. To address this alarming situation and support countries in building resilient malaria programs, the WHO has recently published new guidelines and a framework: a new strategy for malaria-free sub-Saharan Africa; a new effort to control the spread of *Anopheles stephensi* in urban areas."

translations.source: umhlahlandlela omusha, uthuthukiswe ndawonye yiWHO neUN-Habitat, ukuyalela babaholi bedolobha ekulawulweni kwemalalaeveva edolobheni. ithuluzi elisha ukusiza amazwe ahlola umalaleveva wabio wezinhlelo zokubheka nokuhlonza izindawo zotshalomali I-WHO iphinde yanyusa ukwethembeka, ukubalula nokuthola iziphakamiso zemalaleveva. Ukuhlanganiswa kwemiyalelo yeWHO kwamalaleveva sekukhona manje ngezinkundla ezimbili zedijithali: iMAGICapp kanye ne-"MalariaToolkit" isisetshenziswa. I-WHO igqugquzela amazwe ukuthi ahlanganise kuziphakamiso ekuhlelweni kwezifo zendawo kumthelela omkhulu.
translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.

"A new guideline, developed jointly by the WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in the management of urban malaria. The new tool helps countries assess the bio-malaria of surveillance and screening programs for agricultural areas. WHO has also increased the reliability, calculation, and obtaining of malaria risk recommendations. The integration of WHO's malaria guidelines is now available through two digital platforms: the MAGICapp and the "MalariaToolkit" are being used. WHO encourages countries to integrate into recommendations in the planning of local diseases for a greater impact."

translations.source: Izindlela zokungenelela okusha Ukuqhubeka kotshalomali ekuthuthukisweni nokubekwa kwemigomo emisha kamalaleveva namathuluzi esizukulwane esilandelayo kuzobalulekaukuthola okuqondwe ngomalaleveva emhlabeni 2030. Umgomo wesibili kamalaleveva, umgomo iR21/Matrix-M (R21), uma ugunyaziwe ungasiza ukuvala isikhala esikalekayo phakathi kwesaplayi mokufuneka nokuqhubeka ukwehlisa ukugula kwezingane nokufa okudalwa umalaleveva. Kuhamba phambili kuWHO ukuqhubeka ngokubheka kangoti kweR21 malaleveva ngesikhathi sekwengezwe okuphepha okubalulekile,nemininingo kusuka ekuqhubekeni kukaR21 isigaba 3 sesikhona nokinikezelwa kuWHO. Isigaba sokukhiqiza iRTS,S umgomo wamalaleveva emazweni engeziwe eAfrika kulindeleke ukuthi aqale ngokuqala kuka-2024.
translations.reference: Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

**Ways to Intervene with Innovation**

The continuation of the malaria vaccine in the development and deployment of new malaria and 21st-century tools will be crucial to achieving the understanding of malaria globally in 2030. The second malaria vaccine goal, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) goal, if approved, can help close the expected gap between supply and demand and continue to reduce childhood illness and deaths caused by malaria. WHO is moving forward with continued review of the R21 vaccine as additional important safety data and feedback from the ongoing R21 Phase 3 trial and WHO funding are added. The stage of producing RTS,S, a malaria vaccine in more African countries, is expected to begin in early 2024.

translations.source: Endaweni yokulawula ivekitha,kukhona imikhiqizoemisha engu-28 kokuzayo kweR&D. Amathuluzi asabhekisiswa afka, isibonelo, izinhlobo ezintsha zamanethi okulapha izinambuzane, izicuphomziqonde ukudonsa omiyane, isipeshali ripheleli, okwasendlini, (i-eaves tubes) nobunjiniyela bofuzo lwemiyane. Abacwaningi bahambisa phambili ukuthuthuka kokulashwa okungeyona i-ACTS emikhakheni wemithi omele umalaleveva ngenxa yokuphuthuma nokusabalala kokungavuminokuncane kwe-artemisinin. Ukdevelophwa kwemithi yesizukulwane esilandelayo, njenge ACTS, okusebenzisa ukuhlanganiswa kwe-artemisinin nezidakamizwa zimbili, kusendleleni kuzokwehlisa ingcuphe yokungavumi kwesidakamizwa. Amanothi aya kumhleli: ngolwazi olwengeziwe ekukhankaseni ngoSuku lukamalaleveva eMhlabeni lweWHO, vakashela u-http://www.who.int/campains/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"In the vector control management area, there are 28 upcoming R&D products. Tools under review include, for example, new types of mosquito nets, traps designed to attract mosquitoes, a complete specialist, clinical (eaves tubes), and engineering of mosquito drainage. Researchers are driving the development of non-ACT treatments in drug sectors to address malaria due to the emergence and spread of artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation drugs, such as ACTs, using the combination of artemisinin and two other drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes for the editor: for more information when promoting World Malaria Day of WHO, visit http://www.who.int/campains/world-malaria-day/2023"

translations.source: Uhlelo loKudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe i-mRNA iya esigabeni esilandelayo ekuthuthukeni kwayo Ukwethulwa kwehabhu yezobuchwepheshe imRNA indawo e-Afrigen, eNingizimu Afrika. Ababambisani bahlangane eKapa ukuxoxa ngesigaba esilandelayo semRNA umgomo ngokuthuthukisa nokwabelana ngolwazi. Ngaphezu kwababambiqhaza abangu-200 bamazwe angaphandle abasebenza ngoHlelo loKudlulisela lezoBuchwepheshe beMRNA abahlangane eKapa kulelisonto ukuze bahlangane bebonana. Ukujoyinwa iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) uMqondisi Jikelele uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus noDkt Joe Phaahla uNgqongqoshe weZempilo noNgqongqoshe Wezimboni nokuhweba uMnumzane Ebrahim waseNingizimu Afrika ndawonye nezikhulu ezisezingeni eliphezulu eziphuma emazweni axhasile, lomhlangano owehlukile ubheke ukuqhubeka kusukela ukwethula kweWHO neMedicines Patent Pool (MPP) ngoNhlangulana 2021.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

"The mRNA Technology Transfer Programme is moving to the next phase in its development. The launch of the mRNA technology hub is taking place in Afrigen, South Africa. Partners met in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA programme, focusing on development and knowledge sharing. In addition to the 200 international participants working within the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme who gathered in Cape Town this week to meet and network, the World Health Organization (WHO) Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, South Africa’s Minister of Health and Minister of Industry and Trade, Mr. Ebrahim, joined senior officials from supporting countries. This unique meeting aims to build on the WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) announcement in July 2021."

translations.source: "Ngiyajabula ukuba lapha eKapa kanye nababambisani bethu ukuzokweseka imodeli egcinekayo yokudluliselwa kobuchwepheshe mRNA ukunikeza amazwe anemali engenayo encane kuya kwephakathi nendawo ukuthola ngokulingana umgomo kanye neminye imikhiqizo yezempilo esindisa izimpilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngijabule kakhulu ngokuzuziwe kubona bonke ababambe iqhaza kulephrojekthi,ngaphansi kweminyaka emibili sesikhombisile ukuthi uma sisebenza ngokubambisana, siphumelela sonke." Ababambiqhaza bomhlangano kufaka ababambisani bomkhiqizo wasemazweni angu-15 oHlelweni, ongoti abaphambili, imboni abameli bomphakathi kanye nabaxhasimali. Ngesikhathi somhlangano wezinsuku esinhlanu, ababambiqhaza bazokwabelana ngokuqhubeka baxoxe ngokubucayi kwezinto zokugcineka koHlelo njengezinkinga zegunyabunikazi nezindlela zokulawula kanyenesayensi yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA nokusebenza okubalulekile okuhlobene neLMIC kwezinye izndawo zezifo njengeHIV nesifo sofuba. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 sekukale ngaphansi ukuthi ukungalingani okuphelele kukhona ekutholeni imikhiqizo yezempilo, ikakhulukazi imigomo.
translations.reference: “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for the transfer of mRNA technology to provide low- and middle-income countries with equitable access to vaccines and other life-saving health products," said the WHO Director-General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "I am very pleased to acknowledge all those involved in this project; within less than two years, we have demonstrated that when we work together, we all succeed." Participants in the meeting include product partners from 15 countries in the Program, leading scientists, industry representatives, and donors. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and critically discuss key aspects of the Program’s sustainability, such as regulatory hurdles and approaches to science and technology of mRNA and the practical application related to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has further revealed that there is a profound inequity in access to health products, particularly vaccines."

translations.source: Kusukela ngoNdasa 2023, ngaphezu kweminyaka emithathu ngemva kokuthi Iwho imemezele iCOVID-19 njengePublic Health of International Concern (PHEIC), u-69.7% wesibalo sabantu emhlabeni sebethole okungenani idosi eyodwa yomgomo weCOVID-19. Okuqaphelekayo lengxenye izohlala ingaphansi kuka-30% emazweni anemali engenayo encane (amaLIC). Loli Hlelo luhlose ukuxhasa ekutholeno okulinganayo emigomweni ye-mRNA ngokunyusa ukuhambisa kwamandla okukhiqiza okugcinekayo kuwona wonke amaLMIC, ukwandisa okwesifunda nokubambisana kwaphakathi nezifunda nokudevelopha nokusiza ukusebenza kwasendaweni ngokwenziwa kokuqeqeshwa nokwesekwa ngongoti. Yikuphi okwehlukile kumodeli yoKudluliselwa kweZobuchwepheshe kwe-mRNA kuhlelo olunezinto eziningi oluvumela ukwabelana ngobuchwepheshe,ukuthola okuningi ukuze ukukhiqizwa ngokwesifunda nendawo kulabo abadingayo kungafinyelela ngokuphindiwe. UDkt Phaahla, uNgqongqoshe weZempilo eNingizimu Afrika, uthe: "Esikubona namhlanje lapha,umzumzu womlando, uHlelo oluhlose ukusiza iLMIC ngohlelokuxhumana lokubambisana emhlabeni.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

"Starting in August 2023, more than three years after the WHO declared COVID-19 as a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the world's population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. This coverage will remain below 30% in low-income countries (LICs). This Program aims to support equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the sustainable manufacturing capacity transfer to all low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), expanding regional and inter-regional collaboration, and supporting technology transfer and local production through training and mentorship. What is unique about the mRNA Technology Transfer Model is a multi-stakeholder program that allows for technology sharing, increased access to manufacturing for regional and local production for those in need, and repeated access. Dr. Phaahla, the Minister of Health in South Africa, said: "What we are seeing today here is a historic moment, a Program designed to help LMICs through a global partnership network."

translations.source: Ngiyajabula ukubona inqubekela phambili eyenziwe esikhathini esifishane kangaka, siyakwamukela ukwesekwa okusuka emazweni amaningi ahlukene-amazwe afana neNingizimu Afrika anokukhiqizwa okuqinile anamandla, futhi sizimisele ukusebenza ndawonye, ukufunda nokwabelana kuthina sonke." Ungqongqoshe Blade Nzimande weMfundo ePhakeme neSayensi, uthe: "Umbono wethu weHabhu yoKudlulisela ubuchwepheshe be-mRNA kungaphezu kweCOVID-19 nje. Amandla okuthi siyakha afuna usizo kuthina ukubhekana nobhubhane lwesikhathi esizayo imigomo yalo engasebenzisa inkundla efanayo yobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Kungumbono osubeke amehlo awo ekubhekaneni nezifo ezivamile ezindaweni zethu njengoMalaleveva, isifo sofuba, iHIV ne-AIDS." Lomcimbi uphinde umake uhambo olubalulekile loHlelo nokwethulwa kwendawo yeHabhu yobuchwepheshe ne-mRNA e-Afrigen eKapa kukhona uDkt Tedros noDkt Matshidiso Moeti weWHO Regional Director for Afrika, Charles Gore uMqondisi oMkhulu weMPP kanye noNgqongqoshe abahlonishwayo ababamba iqhaza ekusikweni kweribhini emcimbini e-Afrigen.
translations.reference: I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu text:

"I am delighted to see the progress made in such a short time, we welcome the support from many different countries - countries like South Africa which have strong production capabilities, and we are determined to work together, to learn and share with each other." Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science said: "Our vision for the mRNA technology transfer hub is beyond COVID-19 only. The capabilities we are building require assistance from us to deal with future pandemics, the goals of which can utilize the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has its eyes on dealing with common diseases in our regions such as Malaria, tuberculosis, HIV and AIDS." The event also marked a significant milestone in the establishment of the mRNA technology hub program and the launch of the Afrigen mRNA technology hub in Cape Town, where Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore, Director General of MPP, and esteemed Ministers participated in the ribbon-cutting ceremony at Afrigen.

translations.source: USolwazi Petro Terblance, uMqondisi oMkhulu we-Afrigen, uthe: "Iqembu lonke le-Afrigen liyajabula ukufika kulokhu okubalulekile nokuqedwa kwenkundla yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Lenkundla inendawo ekupheleni kokuthuthukiswa komgomo mRNA nokukhiqizwa kwendawo lapho ihabhu ye-mRNA yomgomo weCOVID-19 ikhandidethi AfriVac 2121 njengamanje isezingeni lesikalo esiphezulu. Ngaphezu kwezinyanga ezingu-18 Afrigen isihambe uguquko olukhulu ngokwesekwa kohlelokuxhumana lababambisani nabeluleki abenza loluHlelo. Sesikhulise amandla ethu nokukwazi ukuhlangabezana nezinga elihle eliphezulu lokuthuthukiswa komgomo we-mRNA, ukusiza injongo yokwakha ukukwazi okugcinekile kumaLMIC ukukhiqiza imigomo ye-mRNA.". I-Afrigen isisungulwe ngempumelelo uhlelo lokukhiqizwa kwemigomo yeCOVID-19 ngesikalo selebhu njengamanje kuyanyuka uhlelo ngezinga elifanele lokukhiqiza ngezigaba imigomo ukuthi isetshenziswe ngesigaba sesibili noma sokuqala sokuhlola kwezokwelapha kumazinga eGMP.
translations.reference: Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

Professor Petro Terblance, the CEO of Afrigen, said: "The entire Afrigen team is delighted to reach this significant milestone and the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform has a space at the end of mRNA vaccine development and production where the mRNA vaccine candidate for COVID-19, AfriVac 2121, is currently at a high-level stage. For over 18 months, Afrigen has made significant progress with the support of a network of partners and experts driving this program. We have strengthened our capabilities and the ability to meet the high level of vaccine development, helping to achieve sustainable capacity for LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines." Afrigen has successfully established a system for producing COVID-19 vaccines at the laboratory level and is now scaling up the system to an appropriate production level in stages to be used in the second or first phase of clinical trials at GMP levels.

translations.source: Kuhlelo oluhambisanayo, i-Afrigen izoqhubeka nokwenza ukuqeqesha nokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe kubabambisani bohlelokuxhumana. Abaxhasi badlala indima ebalulekile ekwesekeni uHlelo ngesamba soxhaso kuze kube yimanje ngezigidi ezingu-117 neFrance ekubeni ngeyokuqala ukuxhasa umsebenzi wokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Ababekhona emhlanganweni bekungabamele abaphuma e-European Commission, iBelgium, iGermany kanye neNorway, iCanada, i-Africa Union, iNingizimu Afrika kanye ne-ELMA Foundation. UCaroline Delany, uMqondisi Jikelele weGlobal Affairs Canada waseSouthern and Eastern Afrika, uthe: "iCanada iqinisekisa ukuqgubeka nokweseka kwe-mRNA Technology Transfer Programme futhi siyajabula ukumemezela ukuqhubeka kokunikelwa kwezigidi ezingu-15 ngamadola aseCanada emhlanganweni namhlanje. Lokhu kuletha iminikelo eyisamba yeCanada kuHlelo lwezigidi ezingu-45 kumadola aseCanada.
translations.reference: In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Zulu document:

In the partnership program, Afrigen will continue to provide training and transfer of technical expertise to partner organizations. Supporters play a crucial role in supporting the Program with a contribution of up to $117 million, with France being the first to support the work of transferring mRNA technical expertise. Those present at the meeting represented individuals from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany and Norway, Canada, the Africa Union, South Africa and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, Director General of Global Affairs Canada for Southern and Eastern Africa, said: "Canada reaffirms its commitment and support for the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are pleased to announce the continuation of a contribution of $15 million Canadian dollars at today’s meeting. This brings Canada’s total contributions to the Program to $45 million Canadian dollars."
